Sentence: You push the greenhouse door open and a breath of brine and warm compost meets you like an old friend.
The smell folds around your shoulders—wet wood, kelp-sweet air, the faint metallic tang of salt—and for
a second the town’s noise falls away.
Your boots make the familiar hollow sound on the raised ferry-floor, and the world narrows to seedlings and glass and the worn strap of your sketchbook at your hip.
late sun pouring through translucent solar film, gilding water droplets on leaves so they look like tiny lanterns.
The glass panes carry fingerprints and the ghost of last winter’s tarps.
Somewhere
in a corner, a rainwater barrel tilts a soft metallic tone when a drip finds the rim.
It’s a small orchestra of domestic survival; it steadies something in your chest.
Kai Navarro is hunched over a tray of kelp starters, his sleeves rolled to the elbow.
The solar-fabric overalls are patched—bright squares of cloth that look like sprigs of laughter stitched into his workwear.
He hums
under his breath, an improvisation that smells faintly of mint from the cloth tied to his wrist.
When he looks up, his smile softens into that open, easy expression he uses with everyone here.
It’s the
kind of smile that makes a room feel smaller, in a good way.
Hey.
You made it.' (He scrubs his hands on a rag, then gestures to an empty crate.) 'Saved you a spot.
Did you bring that terrifying compass of yours?
Your fingers move to the brass pendant without thinking.
The compass is warm from your skin, scuffed where your father must have handled it last.
You close your fingers around it—an old ritual, a pulse of
rain like pebbles, the point when the boat lists, your father shouting against wind you still can’t reconstruct.
Saying it
aloud feels like breaking a shell; keeping it in keeps you whole.
You sit, your sketchbook heavy against your thigh.
The page welcomes you; pencil leaves a soft, sure line.
You begin to sketch a breakwater—woven biopolymer ribs knitting like ribs of a shell—imagining it planted not as
careful, practical, shaped by a lifetime of
fitting small pieces into bigger forms.
Kai Navarro watches for a beat, then leans closer, a conspiratorial gleam.
You like that he leans.
People keep shape with each other that way—curves that fit together.
Show me.' (He reaches for the page but pauses, letting you guide his hand away.) 'Tell me where the kelp lines go.
Where the access channels are.
Where kids can still fish without stepping on the reef.
porous layers, pockets for eelgrass, a shallow stair of broken
shells where crabs and memory can return.
Kai Navarro asks questions that are not only technical.
He asks how the structure will feel from the path, from someone’s front step.
He cares about edges, thresholds, the
way a place meets a person.

Conversation grows; it curls outward into other things—the old lighthouse’s recent repairs, a map of the rooftop orchards, a stray thought about Nita’s new batch of smoked fish at the market tomorrow.
Each thread is a
stitch in the neighborhood net your hands learned to weave years ago.
The greenhouse keeps that net taut; the plants and the people who tend them are the knots.
From the doorway, Nita’s voice booms—warm, a little theatrical as ever.
She stands with a crate of newly sprouted marshflowers balanced on one hip, hair bandana bright against dusk.
Mara!
(she teases) You act like you haven’t been gone for a day.' (She sets the crate down and breathes in.) 'Gods, it smells like you’ve been living inside a poem.
Or compost.
Both good things.
You smile, and the sound that escapes you is smaller than a laugh but big in your chest.
Nita’s presence always widens the greenhouse; she has that gravity.
She drops an elbow on the workbench and looks at your sketchbook.
Ooh.
New designs?
You always draw like you’re trying to teach stubborn rocks how to be friendly.' (She points with a finger darkened by soil.) 'Is this for Sol’s meeting on Saturday?
The Reverend will love anything you tie to history.
Mention of Reverend Sol flips a soft switch.
He is steady as tide-stone, a voice that reminds the town of promises made and songs sung at the harbor.
If anyone can make the community feel seen,
it’s him.
You picture him later, cane carved with sea motifs, calling everyone to the raised platforms for a meeting.
Your stomach loosens a little at the thought of steady hands and remembered names.
You trace a shaded arc on the page, imagining how the living seam meets the promenade.
The pencil digs a little too hard.
You feel the brass compass, hot and steady at your throat, as though
salt-bitten, patient.
You haven’t said his name aloud in a long time; in this place it feels less
like a wound and more like an instruction.
He would have liked that,' he says quietly, not prying.
(He steadies a tray, then looks back at you.) 'Your father—he taught you to read water.
You read it like a map.
You make plans that don’t forget people.
There’s an ease in Kai Navarro’s phrasing that keeps grief from coagulating into guilt.
It lets you breathe with the past in one hand and the future in the other.
You nod.
The light slants through
the greenhouse and gilds your shoulder.
You feel small and enormous at once—small in your solitary memory, enormous in the fact of community hands around you.
• Set the sketchbook down and let Kai rearrange the kelp starters
You hand him the page.
He studies the lines, fingers tracing your marks as if they were topography, then hums and suggests a slight curve to the outer ribs—practical, kind.
• Tuck the sketchbook closer and keep drawing alone
You pull the page away with a soft smile, adding a tiny annotation about access points.
Kai watches for a heartbeat, then returns to his seedlings with a small, approving nod.
Continue to the following shared narration.
The greenhouse fills with the soft, companionable work of two people who know how to share an idea without swallowing each other.
You and Kai Navarro speak in interrupted sentences, crossing into each other’s thoughts and
finishing the other’s measurements, a duet of tools and proposals.
Each exchange leaves you warmer; hope doesn’t need fireworks here—just patient labor and mutual translation.
The sun slides lower.
Outside, the sky goes the color of kelp in shadow; inside, the glass takes on that same green-gold, making every leaf seem worth fighting for.
Somewhere outside the building, a child’s laughter threads into the air—a reminder that this is for them, too.
Reverend Sol enters with slow, deliberate steps, cane tapping in a rhythm that somehow belongs to the tide.
He carries a thermos, the metal dented, and when he settles he speaks with the soft authority of someone who has shepherded storms.
Reverend Sol Mara, good to see you.' (His eyes find yours with a warmth that does not ask for explanations.) 'The town needs people like you.
Steady hands.
Stubborn hearts.
You feel the words land like pebbles smoothing under water.
Flattery is seldom his language; his praise feels like permission.
You set down the sketchbook; Sol peers at the drawings with the curiosity of someone who remembers both maps and songs.
Reverend Sol This living seam—how will folks get to the shore when we need them?
How does it sit with the old docks?
You answer, patient, detailing the gentle approach, the places you have allowed for crab pots and children with nets.
You speak of hands and thresholds, not just bolts and concrete.
Sol nods, thoughtful.
Reverend Sol Good.
Keep the thresholds.
Keep the music.' (He smiles, then glances at Kai Navarro.) 'And you, lad, don’t forget to listen when she says 'no more quick fixes.' She knows the town the way the sea knows its own markings.
The exchange is small but it lays another plank across the gap between memory and plan.
You like the feeling of being seen without having to explain the whole past.
It allows grief to soften into resolve—an ember coaxed into a steady, useful glow.
Juno slips in, cheeks flushed, hoodie streaked with paint.
The kid flings a grin and a half-formed question that becomes a comment about a spray-painted bicycle.
You laugh—Juno’s presence
is an immediate, bright reminder of what’s at stake.
You imagine a future where they can ride the raised platforms without fearing sudden floods.
The image steadies you.
Tea?' she asks again, pressing a chipped mug into your hand.
'It’s that ginger-lemon you like.
It’ll make your sketches taste better.
that the town feeds people who are building the town.
• Sip the tea slowly, savoring the calm
You lift the mug, the warmth unfurling through your fingers.
The tea tastes like home and makes your shoulders un-tangle.
When you set it down, your palm leaves a crescent of steam on the wood.
• Offer the mug to Juno to hide nerves
You hand it to Juno with a teasing smile.
They take it with a mock bow, and the three of you exchange a small laugh that breaks something tight in your chest.
Continue to the following shared narration.
The light outside finally hushes into dusk.
Inside, the greenhouse keeps a gentle pulse of life—irrigation, breathing, small talk about seed counts and the Reverend’s next potluck.
You flip to a fresh page and write a
'community access + living modules + teach apprentices.' It’s the way you give form to hope; small commitments that add up.
a curving
path that leads people, not away from the sea, but into a stewardship of it.
Kai Navarro watches you sign the page with a tiny wave symbol.
He tilts his head, soft as a question.
Are you… all right?
Not just tonight—are you holding up?
you’re tired, yes; afraid sometimes, yes; but surrounded, too.
You are held by faces and hands and plans.
Saying it aloud makes it solid.
I am.
I’m tired, but—' (You look around at the greenhouse, at the people who make it a net rather than a wall.) '—I’m not alone.
And that changes things.
Kai Navarro’s smile widens in a hush, the kind that says he’s counting faith with you.
Outside, the first stars prick the sky; inside, the greenhouse holds its human constellation steady.
Hope is not a flag
planted in the sand.
It’s a small ember you tend slowly—adding paper, a controlled breath, a steady hand.
The evening deepens into that gentle, resilient quiet that comes after work is done but before sleep.
You feel the town’s patchwork repairs like a warm net around your battered resolve, every thread a neighbor’s small contribution.
You close the sketchbook and slide it into its leather strap, the page inside stained with a circle of tea and several smudged notes.
You stand, feeling the day settle in your bones, the plans heavy
and possible.
There will be meetings and voices and the hard teeth of negotiation in the weeks to come, but tonight the seed is set.
You step toward the door with the others trailing behind, a little line of everyday procession.
The greenhouse light leaves a rectangle on the damp planks, a threshold between the safe warmth and the cool air
that tomorrow holds the Tideward meeting where plans will stretch into public hearing.
For now, the ember is steady.
You carry it
with you like a small lamp.
You step out from the Saltflower Greenhouse carrying the warmth of the meeting there like a coal in your palm.
The promenade smells of brine and old rope; salt clings to your jacket collar.
People have
gathered in the shallow amphitheater of raised platforms, folding chairs pulled into an uneven semicircle.
At the center, a temporary lectern stands beside a projection screen patched with translucent solar film where Blackbrand’s renderings will be
shown.
You press your palm flat to the brass compass at your throat for a fraction of a second—its cool ring a private anchor.
Around you, faces you know settle into the inevitable hush of a town
expectant, wary, tired but ready.
Kai slips into the row beside you, his presence a low, steady warmth.
He gives you a small nod, then points discreetly at the screen as technicians boot the presentation.
You return the nod, and in that simple exchange there’s a thousand tiny negotiations about what compromises you will or will not make.
Good evening, Marisport.
I'm Evelyn Black of Blackbrand Adaptive Systems.
We are here to offer protection—rapidly deployable, modular barriers that will hold against projected storm surges for decades.
steel-faced segments nesting together, walkways built atop the barrier, sensors embedded at intervals.
The renderings gleam where the real world is matte: her images promise order.
These modules can be installed in phases, prioritized by risk, and they're engineered to integrate with existing port infrastructure.
They’re cost-effective, and—' (she pauses, letting the word land) '—they save lives.
A ripple of polite applause.
You watch the crowd as if reading the tide—who leans toward fear, who toward skepticism.
Some homeowners glance at their shoes, measuring mud and mortgage.
A few fishermen cluster like a small shoal behind the front row, eyes narrowed.
The engineering is remarkable—but it's not the whole picture.' (She steps up, tablet in hand.) 'Living systems respond differently.
Oysters, eelgrass, kelp—they build resilience over time and provide ecological services that hard structures don't.
I have monitoring data from pilot plots: wave attenuation increases meaningfully after two seasons; biodiversity rebounds; sediment stabilizes.
data first, rhetoric later.
She slides a graph onto the screen—lines, confidence intervals—then a photo of a kelp line bending like a chorus in the surf.
People
lean forward; some wrinkle their brows at the small print, others visibly absorb the image of living reef.
We need to make that data feel immediate.
Translate it—how much shoreline did those pilot plots save?
How long to scale?
If we can show timelines and costs next to her phasing, people will stop feeling like it’s between two absolutes.
You consider that.
The impulse to stand and speak is sharp—words shaped by late nights sketching reef layouts and writing grant letters.
But Reverend Sol clears his throat, and the crowd calms in deference.
Reverend Sol Marisport has always weathered storms together.
We owe ourselves deliberation that honors bodies and histories, not only spreadsheets.
Let truth and memory sit at this table—let us not decide in haste for fear.
the shrimping families who left, the house with its porch posts now bay-bottom.
Nita, apron still in place though the
market closed hours ago, stands with a hand on her hip.
Her jaw sets with that honest ferocity you know well.
I'm not saying no to protecting lives.
I'm saying don't sell our people for renderings.
Who pays if your plan raises property values and pushes long-time renters out?
Who pays when the river of tourists follows a safe promenade and rents spike?
We've seen this pattern.
Murmurs swell—anger threaded with fear.
A woman two rows ahead wipes at her eyes.
An older man laughs once, sharp and disbelieving.
Evelyn's expression is controlled; you can't tell whether she’s weighing compromise or simply cataloguing opposition.
Displacement is a real concern.
Blackbrand has models for affordable housing offsets and community spaces integrated into the wall.
We can designate funds—
Nita interrupts, voice rough Funds in a binder don't shelter a grandmother from eviction notices next winter.
We need guarantees, land trusts, ownership.
We need to know whose lives matter.
The exchange tenses; you feel the heat of it along your spine as if the sun itself leaned in.
• Ask Evelyn about concrete lifecycle and maintenance
You raise your hand and, when acknowledged, ask about the long-term maintenance burdens and whose budgets would shoulder them—engineering costs, replacement cycles, who pays when a module fails.
• Challenge displacement plan and demand binding commitments
You stand and press Evelyn on binding protections for renters and small owners—what legal guarantees will Blackbrand attach to avoid speculative displacement?
The meeting continues, discussion moves to comparing proposed models.
You opt for neither immediately, letting others press first.
Your restraint is tactical; you need more than rhetoric to shift this meeting.
When a council member opens the floor for short rebuttals, Dr.
Leila steps forward again.
If we're comparing lifespans and costs, please include ecosystem services.
Kelp and oyster reefs sequester carbon, support fisheries, and lower insurance costs by reducing peak wave energy.
These are quantifiable benefits that accrue to the whole town.
Evelyn responds with the same polished calm that’s been her hallmark all evening.
Quantification is possible—but it takes time and coordination.
Our proposal offers a bridge.
We are not opposed to integrating living elements; we're offering assurance now.
compressed choices, compressed futures.
You have the credibility, Mara.
People trust you.
If you propose a demonstration—one measurable module of living shoreline paired with transparent cost accounting, staged with clear milestones—it could change the tenor.
It gives everyone a thing to point to, increases trust.
possibility wrapped in logistics.
You also feel the old guilt, the one that sits where your ribs meet your sternum—your father’s
absence in a storm still a cold echo in your decision-making.
You do not want to trade one kind of safety for another that erodes the town’s fabric.
You stand, and when you speak your voice is steadier than you feel.
People of Marisport—what if we don't have to choose between speed and care?
Blackbrand, show us your phasing and funding models.
We'll propose a parallel: a public demonstration of scaling living systems—kelp lines and oyster reefs—paired with a community-funded monitoring hub.
Let's map the timelines side-by-side and fund transparent, legally binding protections for renters and small owners as a condition for any corporate contract.
Conversation splinters around you—some applauding, some skeptical.
Evelyn inclines her head, an inscrutable gesture.
I appreciate the spirit.
Blackbrand is open to pilots.
But clarity is necessary.
Public pilots can be costly and delay deployment.
When the sea comes earlier than models predict, delay costs lives.
Her words are not a threat so much as a cold, rational warning.
You feel no dramatic lightning, only the slow tightening of choices.
We can design an experimental phase that’s rigorous and fast.
Monitoring methodologies exist.
If Blackbrand is willing to commit to timelines and to fund oversight, we can begin within months.
Reverend Sol rises, cane tapping softly in rhythm with his sentences.
Reverend Sol If we move forward with both—test and structure—we must cement community governance.
A board, binding agreements, emergency clauses.
Not only engineers and contracts—folks who live here should steward what we do.
Let us not hand over our shore.
Nita is unrelenting.
I want the paperwork.
I want trustees.
I want housing protections before any shovel hits mud.
I want guarantees, not promises.
People cry for clarity and protection.
The meeting is not so much an argument as a sieve; facts, fears, and hopes sift through, leaving a residue that looks like policy in formation.
Kai squeezes your hand once—brief, encouraging.
He’s already thinking of outreach, of leaflets, of translating those monitoring charts into something a neighbor can parcel and understand.
If you push this, Mara, I'll help build the pilot.
Leila will give the science.
I know people with boats, and Sol can help with community governance.
We can make a small, fast project that proves scale.
a demonstration that can be done on a tightened timeline, with clear metrics, with binding protections.
It feels plausible, and dangerous, and necessary.
You picture the kelp lines
dropping into brackish water, tiny green hands catching the surf, and below that the ledger of who owns which plot, written into a trust.
Your internal checklist runs fast—permits, sensors, volunteers, outreach.
You picture Juno's face if you fail and the quiet relief if you do not.
Responsibility burrows deep; you inhale the cold, briny air as if it will give you the courage to figure the rest out.
Evelyn watches you from the edge of the stage; her face gives nothing away—complex, unreadable in that way that keeps all past interactions uncommitted.
• Call for an immediate pilot committee meeting after the forum
You propose an adjacent meeting tonight—field scientists, Blackbrand reps, community trustees—to hammer out terms and timelines.
It signals urgency and keeps momentum alive.
• Ask the crowd for a show of hands to gauge support for a pilot
a raw, visible measure that will shape pressure on the council and Blackbrand alike.
The crowd breaks into smaller groups to plan next steps; discussion and planning continue.
A woman near the back shouts, 'We need action, not more meetings!' The cry is a pitched wire across the crowd—anger and exhaustion braided.
Reverend Sol raises a palm and the room settles, the way a harbor settles under wind.
Reverend Sol Let us record the questions and answer them honestly.
Let us scope the pilot and set community criteria.
Let us be clear what we will not cede.
small, practical actions stitched into policy.
The surge of adrenaline you felt earlier recedes into a steady, mid-level focus.
This is the arousal of doing: not panic, not triumph—methodical, deliberate increase toward a plan.
Evelyn steps down from the lectern in the soft light, and people begin to drift into clustered conversations—some leaning toward Blackbrand reps, others toward Dr.
Leila and your group.
The tide breathes against the pilings; seagulls wheel and call, indifferent to municipal urgency.
Kai pulls you aside for a breath.
You did that well.
People listened.
Whatever comes next, we need to nail the first ninety days—timeline, budget, transparency.
If we can get that written with legal teeth, Nita's worries get smaller.
You nod.
Your mind is already composing the first page of that ninety-day plan—who to call, what gear you'd need, which volunteer divers could be trusted with sensor deployment.
You taste salt and the faint metal
tang of adrenaline, not sharp but steady, like a tideline you are learning to read.
You fold your hands around your compass, feeling its edges like a promise.
Overhead, the first high clouds thin and let a thread of violet sky show.
The meeting breaks into smaller urgencies and promises to
reconvene.
The crowd disperses in little groups—some angry, some hopeful, some determined to wait for data.
You walk the boardwalk with Kai at your side, the conversation following, dissolving into planning and the pragmatic logistics of a pilot.
a proposal, a counterproposal, an agreement to try.
The arousal that carried you through the meeting remains steady, focused on the work: that slow, necessary climb toward demonstration.
You feel the town hinge on the next step.
You feel, too, the private weight of your father's memory—an old compass by your chest—reminding you that choices made now will be felt for years.
The lamp-light along the promenade bends and pools; you and Kai pause where the boards meet the water's edge.
Behind you, voices carry—plans scaffolded into motion.
Ahead, the sea glints, patient and implacable.
the plan must be built, the pilot funded, the protections written.
You already know some of the first calls you will make.
You breathe in salt and ledger-paper and possibility, and then step into what comes next.
You step off the promenade and onto the market planks with your sketchbook heavy at your hip and the compass warm against your sternum.
The smell hits you first—frying oil, damp kelp, hot metal from nearby gear, and the unmistakable brine that tastes like history and threat at once.
steady, carved cane in
hand, his voice trying to hold a wavering sea of anger and fear.
Nita is two stalls over, apron gone—her hands on her hips, eyes hard as ballast.
Juno stands by her, arms folded, chin set;
the kid keeps glancing at you as if trying to measure whether safety still exists within your shoulders.
because the town wants something from you now—direction, a plan, a name for the fear.
Reverend Sol Mara—' (his voice is softer than the crowd; you feel it as if he’s speaking into a fog) '—we can't let this drift.
You understand the soil and the sea.
Speak plainly.
compromises that saved pockets and lost neighborhoods; quick fixes that smoothed paperwork but hollowed out trust.
Each possibility hums with consequence.
Nita intercepts you before you reach the pallets.
Her face is a map of certainty and exhaustion.
You heard Evelyn's plan.
Concrete segments, cranes, lights.
They call it progress.
It will bury half of the eelgrass beds and put a toll on the fishers.
Are you going to stand for that, or are we going to stand for ourselves?
You open your mouth.
You want to list the living-shoreline pilot—kelp lines, oyster clusters, porous berms.
You want to cite Dr.
Leila's tentative models and the idea of scaling slowly.
But the crowd is a clock
counting down to a public hearing, and Evelyn's company is filing permits while you two argue semantics.
• Answer Nita with calm facts
You take a breath and lay out the data—how oyster reefs dissipate wave energy, how kelp lines slow surge.
Nita’s shoulders drop fractionally; she nods but the hard set of her jaw remains.
• Respond with a sharp, emotional plea
You let the ache of your father’s loss thread through your voice.
Your words land like stones—solid, raw.
Nita's eyes flash; she reaches for your arm and squeezes it, anger turned into urgent solidarity.
• Take Evelyn’s offer to speak privately
You nod, step down from the pallets, and inclination pulls you toward a quieter conversation.
Evelyn's aides close ranks politely; the air smells like ozone and polished metal.
The crowd scowls, feeling betrayed by the idea of backroom deals.
• Refuse and stay in public, force transparency
You plant your feet on the pallets and meet Evelyn's gaze across the sea of faces.
People lean in; some cheer, some hiss.
Your refusal tightens the room like a drum.
You step into a room that smells of citrus cleaner and new rubber—a synthetic, antiseptic scent designed to suggest order.
The air feels slightly cooler than the boardwalk; your jacket carries the faint, warmer smells of kelp and wet soil behind you like evidence of another life.
shoulders squared, tablet angled so the light frames her sharp profile.
When she looks up, her slate-blue eyes assess you the way a tide tests a shoreline—measuring retention, erosion, the shape of what might remain.
Kai Navarro stands half behind you, hands folded at his waist like a man trying to keep still when his hands want to build.
Dr.
Leila sits with a small stack of sensor reports fanned beside
her—paper edges softened from handling.
Reverend Sol's cane rests against the table, his face folded in the soft, careful way someone listens for a long story.
Nita's bandana is drawn low; her mouth is a narrow
line.
find whether the
glossy promise Evelyn offers can be made to serve the town without hollowing out its soul.
Evelyn Black gives the slightest tilt of acknowledgment—polite, unreadable.
Thank you all for coming.
Blackbrand understands the urgency Marisport faces.
We've drafted a proposal aimed at protecting the waterfront quickly, while creating public terraces and green corridors—spaces for markets, for gardens.
It's not just concrete; it's modular segments with integrated living terraces.
It's designed to be rapid-deploy, adaptable, and aesthetically...
respectful of local life.
She taps the tablet; a rendered cross-section rises on the screen—sleek concrete modules with planting pockets, stair access to terraces, boat slips preserved in illustrations.
The renderings look expensive and inevitable.
You listen, and your chest tightens.
The word 'rapid' is a cold thing in your ears; it promises safety but also the speed at which decisions can swallow nuance.
Dr.
Leila leans forward, fingers steepled.
Evelyn, the integration of terraces and green pockets is encouraging on paper.
But living shoreline strategies—reefs, oyster beds, kelp lines—function differently.
They rely on biological time and sometimes slower accretion.
We need to see baseline modeling of hydrodynamics with the modules in place, plus long-term monitoring commitments.
Evelyn Black's expression doesn't falter; she has an architecture of composure.
We've run hydrodynamic simulations and partnered with coastal firms; the modules are engineered to reduce surge energy and to allow for habitat pockets.
We're prepared to fund initial monitoring.
What we need from Marisport is a binding implementation agreement so we can secure permits and financing.
You hear 'binding' as if it were a gavel.
Your mouth tastes like old tidewater.
The phrase carries a legal firmness that could lock futures in place before the town has time to breathe.
Binding how?
We can't agree to a contract that doesn't guarantee community governance.
What are the enforcement mechanisms if the terraces are repurposed later?
If leases shift or maintenance is privatized, who keeps the promise?
We're offering a joint oversight board—representation from the municipality, community stakeholders, and Blackbrand.
It will set maintenance schedules and program space.
We can codify certain public uses.
Nita snorts softly, a sound like breaking shells.
You say 'joint,' you say 'codify'—but we're the ones who get pushed out when things get...finished.
I live three blocks from the promenade.
Tell me how I keep my stall when a terrace becomes 'premium vendor space' paid for by corporate contracts.
Reverend Sol folds his hands and speaks slowly, each word deliberately worn.
Reverend Sol Words on glossy paper are not the same as stewardship.
How will you protect low-income residents, long-time renters, and families who can't bid against developers when spaces are monetized?
Protection must mean more than physical slabs.
Evelyn Black's gaze flicks toward him; for a fraction of a second something like regret crosses her features—quick as a gull passing.
Then it hardens.
We can include clauses—priority leasing for legacy vendors, community-run management stipulations, legally enforceable anti-displacement language.
We have lawyers.
We can bind successors to obligations.
loopholes, interpretation, the slow creep of exceptions.
Your father's memory
surfaces—how a single unchecked promise had cost him the sea—and the guilt at the thought of making a similar mistake knots your stomach.
• Interrupt, press Evelyn on legal detail now
You cut across the sheen.
'What specific clause binds successors?
Who enforces it if Blackbrand changes hands?' Your tone is low but sharp, and the table goes quiet.
Evelyn's jaw tightens as she prepares a precise sentence.
• Let her finish the pitch before you challenge
You hold back, letting the renderings finish their sweep across the screen.
Sometimes letting a sales pitch breathe reveals the seams.
Kai arches an eyebrow at you, grateful for the delay.
Evelyn continues, her cadence steady.
• Push for immediate draft review—call for lawyers now
You lock eyes with Evelyn.
'We need the draft in hand before any provisional endorsement.' Your voice is steadier than you feel.
Dr.
Leila nods vigorously; Kai exhales like a man letting a plan exist.
Evelyn's fingers tap the tablet; she accepts the demand with a cool 'we'll do that.'
• Propose a conditional endorsement—buy time for emergency measures
You say, 'We can provisionally endorse a plan that funds immediate protective measures while we hammer out language.' Kai looks at you, relief and worry tangled in his gaze.
Evelyn tilts her head, considering the political advantage of a visible endorsement, then smiles thinly.
You walk in with the weight of the meeting behind your ribs, the pen-suspended hesitation from the municipal chamber still raw on your skin.
Reverend Sol's cane taps a precise rhythm against the metal grating as
he takes the chair at the head.
Lawyers fan red-lined pages like cards.
Evelyn Black is already there, tablet open, calm as a tide that knows the hour it must come in.
We don't have the luxury of open-ended experiments, Mara.
People need protection this season.
The indemnities protect our contractors; they let us move quickly.
You taste acrid coffee and ozone.
The Spire's LED ring throws everyone in clinical light; it picks out the thin blue of Dr.
Leila's glasses, the salt in Kai's braid, and the bandana-worn crease of Nita's
brow.
